The segment opens with a close-up of Molly Kinder, a woman with long, light brown hair, wearing a dark blue blazer over a maroon dress, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit indoor setting. On-screen text identifies her as 'MOLLY KINDER, Senior Fellow, New America' with the 'FRONTLINE' logo. She speaks clearly and articulately about tasks like 'processing information, processing paper, transferring data,' highlighting their 'huge potential for automation,' noting that 'AI is going to do some of that, software um robots are going to do some of that.' The scene then transitions to a wider shot of Molly presenting in front of a large projection screen, where she gestures toward two images: a black-and-white archival photo of women working at a switchboard and a modern industrial facility. She asks, 'So, how many people are still working as switchboard operators? Probably none in this country.' A distinct male voice then interjects, stating, 'The workplace of the future will demand different skills. And gaining them, says Molly Kinder, will depend on who can afford them.' The camera cuts to audience members, including a man in a light blue button-down shirt with glasses and a man in a plaid shirt with a name tag reading 'David', both listening attentively. Molly's voice returns, explaining, 'it's not a good situation in the United States. There's been some excellent research that says that half of Americans couldn't afford a $400 unexpected expense,' adding that for a '$1,000, there's even less.' The video returns to close-ups of Molly, her expression earnest and engaged, as she continues to speak about the inability of people to go 'without a month's pay, two months' pay, a year,' or to put 'savings toward a course to redevelop your career.' She emphasizes that 'People can't afford to take time off of work. They don't have a cushion,' concluding that 'this lack of economic stability married with the disruptions in people's careers is a real...' before the audio cuts off.

event_blocks.summary: An aerial view of a dense urban skyline at dusk, with illuminated skyscrapers, establishes a corporate and financial atmosphere as a narrator states that new machines will penetrate every sector of the economy, from insurance companies to law firms and Wall Street trading floors. The camera zooms into a high-rise, showing office workers at their desks before transitioning to a bustling trading floor where traders, surrounded by monitors displaying financial data and stock tickers, work amidst the ambient sounds of chatter and keyboard clicks. A CNBC graphic for 'BLUE-CHIP RETAIL SHOWDOWN COMING UP' is visible. Steven Berkenfeld, an investment banker identified under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, explains that industries are disrupted by technologies like robotics, drones, and blockchain, which companies use to increase efficiency by reducing the workforce. Brief nighttime exterior shots of the building, harbor, cranes, and boats reinforce the global financial hub setting. Berkenfeld then discusses opportunity in the country, the inequality of opportunity, and the diminishing likelihood of advancing beyond one's parents' socioeconomic status.

Steven Berkenfeld.description: Man with curly white hair, wearing a dark suit, light pink shirt, and a patterned tie, identified as 'Investment banker' by on-screen text under the FRONTLINE logo. He speaks directly to the camera with a serious, professional demeanor, his voice distinct, slightly gravelly, and articulate, discussing disruptive technologies, efficiency, and the inequality of opportunity.

FRONTLINE Logo.description: Bold white 3D text on a red background, appearing in the lower-left corner during the interview scene, indicating the documentary source.

Trading Floor.description: Large, open-plan office filled with traders at desks equipped with multiple computer monitors displaying financial data. Overhead screens show stock tickers and market indices, including a CNBC graphic for 'BLUE-CHIP RETAIL SHOWDOWN COMING UP'. The scene is accompanied by the ambient sounds of a busy trading floor, including indistinct chatter and keyboard clicks.

Financial Data Screens.description: Multiple computer monitors and large wall-mounted screens displaying real-time financial data, including stock indices like S&P 500, NASDAQ, and commodity prices, with green and red numbers indicating market movements.

raw_segments: The segment opens with an aerial view of a dense urban skyline at dusk, showcasing towering skyscrapers with illuminated windows, establishing a corporate and financial atmosphere. A calm, authoritative male voice narrates, stating that "the new machines will penetrate every sector of the economy, from insurance companies to human resource departments, from law firms to the trading floors of Wall Street." The camera zooms into a high-rise building, revealing office workers at their desks, emphasizing late hours, before transitioning to a bustling trading floor. Here, traders are at desks surrounded by multiple monitors displaying financial data, with large screens showing stock tickers and market indices, including a CNBC graphic for 'BLUE-CHIP RETAIL SHOWDOWN COMING UP'. The ambient sounds of a busy trading floor, including indistinct chatter and keyboard clicks, accompany the scene. The focus then shifts to a close-up of Steven Berkenfeld, an investment banker with curly white hair, wearing a dark suit, light pink shirt, and a patterned tie, identified by on-screen text under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. He speaks directly to the camera with a distinct, slightly gravelly voice, explaining how "every industry is going through" disruptive technologies like "robotics or drones or blockchain," and how companies are applying these to "make myself more efficient," which "means mostly how do I do this with fewer workers." The video briefly cuts to nighttime exterior shots of the same building, with glowing windows and a view of a harbor with cranes and boats, suggesting a global financial hub, before returning to Steven Berkenfeld, who continues to discuss "opportunity in this country" and "the inequality of opportunity," and the "likelihood that you won't be able to advance from where your parents were."

event_blocks.summary: A voiceover begins by describing America as the land of opportunity, accompanied by visuals of a man in a dark suit speaking in a modern office with a blurred cityscape. This transitions to a nighttime city street with traffic and pedestrians, overlaid with a Labor and Profit Share Graph showing a significant shift from 1980 to 2015, where a declining blue line representing middle and lower earners' share of wealth crossed a rising red line representing the top 1% of earners' share, indicating increasing inequality. The visuals also include illuminated skyscrapers and a Server Room, highlighting technological and urban development. Jerry Kaplan, identified by the FRONTLINE logo, a computer scientist and entrepreneur with white hair and beard, wearing a dark blazer over a light blue shirt, speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit indoor setting with a blurred domestic background, gesturing expressively as he explains that artificial intelligence, if not thoughtfully implemented, drives increased inequality through automation, which substitutes capital for labor, benefiting those with capital and echoing Karl Marx's theories on the capital-labor struggle.

Server Room.description: Long corridor of black server racks with red indicator lights, creating a high-tech ambiance.

Jerry Kaplan.description: Computer scientist and entrepreneur with white hair and beard, wearing a dark blazer over a light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit indoor setting with a blurred domestic background; the FRONTLINE logo is visible in the lower-left corner. He discusses how artificial intelligence, if not thoughtfully implemented, drives increased inequality by substituting capital for labor, referencing Karl Marx's ideas on the struggle between capital and labor.

FRONTLINE Logo.description: Bold white 3D text on a red background, appearing in the lower-left corner during the interview scene, indicating the documentary source.

Labor and Profit Share Graph.description: Line graph titled 'Labor and Profit Share of US National Income, 1939–2017', showing two lines fluctuating over time, projected on a large screen behind the man in the blue shirt, and also overlaid on urban backdrops. The audio describes the red line as representing the top 1% of earners and the blue line as representing middle and lower earners, noting that the lines crossed 15 years ago, indicating a rise in inequality.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a man in a dark suit and patterned tie speaking in a modern office, with a blurred cityscape in the background, as a male voiceover discusses how America is perceived as the land of opportunity. This transitions to a nighttime city street with traffic and pedestrians, over which a line graph is superimposed, illustrating a significant shift from 1980 to 2015. The graph, which shows one blue line declining and one red line rising, is explained by the voiceover as representing how the top 1% of earners (red) owned a relatively small portion of wealth, while middle and lower earners (blue) had the largest share, until the lines crossed 15 years ago, leading to increasing inequality. These visuals are interspersed with illuminated skyscrapers and a server room, emphasizing technological and urban development. The scene then cuts to Jerry Kaplan, identified by the FRONTLINE logo, a computer scientist and entrepreneur with white hair and beard, wearing a dark blazer over a light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit indoor setting with a blurred domestic background. He gestures expressively as he explains that artificial intelligence, if not thoughtfully implemented, is a driver for increased inequality because it represents automation, which is the substitution of capital for labor, leading to those with capital winning, and referencing Karl Marx's ideas on the struggle between capital and labor.

event_blocks.summary: A man with white hair and a gray beard, dressed in a dark suit and light blue shirt, discusses societal distribution of economic benefits and future moral considerations. Kai-Fu Lee, CEO of Sinovation Ventures, wearing a dark blue suit, light blue shirt, and patterned tie, explains that AI will exacerbate the gap between the rich and poor, potentially tearing society apart. A thoughtful, melancholic instrumental track begins as the scene shifts to a desolate urban street at dawn, showing a 'CLINTON AV' street sign and pedestrians, contrasting with a nighttime political rally where a large crowd cheers and waves 'TRUMP PENCE' and 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN' signs, with some attendees recording on their phones. Donald Trump, at a podium with a 'TRUMP PENCE MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN' sign during his 2016 'Thank You Tour' in Cincinnati, Ohio, thanks the energetic crowd, met with cheers and chants. Harry Cripps, President of UAW Local 668, seated in a union hall with American flags and the FRONTLINE logo, describes the Trump vote as a protest against the current direction and expresses fear for the future due to automation, noting that the wealthy are accumulating all the money while the middle class struggles to support families. The video concludes with a stark image of a desolate urban street at dawn, lined with makeshift tents and debris, featuring a 'BODY OIL' sign, a sharp contrast to the earlier rally and union hall scenes.

Clinton Avenue Street Sign.description: Green street sign reading 'CLINTON AV' visible above a person riding a scooter in an outdoor urban setting.

Man with white hair and beard.description: Man with white hair and a gray beard, wearing a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking seriously in a warmly lit indoor setting, discussing the distribution of economic benefits and future moral considerations for society.

Kai-Fu Lee.description: Man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark blue suit, light blue shirt, and a patterned tie, speaking expressively in a softly lit room with a blurred bookshelf background; the FRONTLINE logo appears in the lower-left corner with his name and title displayed. He discusses how AI will exacerbate the gap between 'haves and have-nots,' potentially tearing society apart, and states that AI's impact will make things worse.

TRUMP PENCE Rally Crowd.description: Large crowd at a nighttime political rally, holding red and white signs reading 'TRUMP PENCE' and 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN', cheering loudly, applauding, and chanting 'USA! USA!' in response to Donald Trump's speech, with some recording the event on their phones, creating an energetic atmosphere.

Desolate Urban Street.description: Empty city street at dawn, lined with makeshift tents and debris, suggesting homelessness, with a 'BODY OIL' sign visible on a building, under an overcast sky, creating a somber and neglected atmosphere.

FRONTLINE Logo.description: Bold white 3D text on a red background, appearing in the lower-left corner during the interview scene, indicating the documentary source.

Harry Cripps.description: Man with a beard and glasses, wearing a navy blue zip-up jacket with 'PRESIDENT' and 'UAW LOCAL 668' embroidered on it, speaking in a calm, reflective, and slightly gravelly voice that becomes more concerned as he discusses the Trump vote as a protest and expresses fear for the future due to automation and economic inequality, all while seated in an empty union hall with stacked chairs and American flags in the background.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a man with white hair and a gray beard, wearing a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking seriously in a warmly lit indoor setting, discussing how society will need to distribute economic benefits and the major moral considerations for the coming decades. The scene then transitions to Kai-Fu Lee, CEO of Sinovation Ventures, identified by on-screen text and wearing a dark blue suit, light blue shirt, and a patterned tie, speaking expressively in a softly lit room with a blurred bookshelf background. He explains that the increasing gap between 'haves and have-nots' will be exacerbated by AI, potentially tearing society apart as the rich gain too much and the poor struggle to escape their situation, concluding that AI's impact will make things worse. As a thoughtful, slightly melancholic instrumental track begins, the video cuts to an outdoor urban street scene with pedestrians walking along a sidewalk lined with shops and parking meters under daylight, where a green 'CLINTON AV' street sign is visible above a person riding a scooter. The final scene shifts to a nighttime political rally, where a large crowd holds up red and white 'TRUMP PENCE' and 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN' signs, cheering and waving energetically to the sound of loud crowd cheers and indistinct shouts, with some attendees recording the event on their phones, creating a charged atmosphere.

The video opens with Donald Trump speaking at a political rally in Cincinnati, Ohio, during his 2016 'Thank You Tour,' standing at a podium with a 'TRUMP PENCE MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN' sign. His voice addresses the energetic crowd, stating, "I'm here today for one main reason. To say thank you to Ohio," which is met with loud cheers, applause, and "USA! USA!" chants from the many attendees wearing red 'Make America Great Again' hats, waving signs, and recording on their phones. A wide shot emphasizes the packed arena before the scene transitions to an interview with Harry Cripps, President of UAW Local 668 in Saginaw, MI. Seated in an empty union hall with stacked chairs and American flags in the background, and with the FRONTLINE logo displayed, Cripps speaks directly to the camera in a calm, reflective, and slightly gravelly voice, explaining, "I think the Trump vote was a protest... it was a protest vote. They didn't like the direction things were going." His voice then shifts to a more concerned tone as he adds, "I'm scared... I worry about the future of not just this country but the entire globe. If we continue to go in an automated system, what are we gonna do? Now I've got a group of people at the top that are making all the money and I don't have anybody in the middle that can support a family." The final segment shifts to a desolate urban street at dawn, lined with makeshift tents and debris, suggesting homelessness, with a 'BODY OIL' sign visible on a building, creating a stark contrast to the earlier rally’s energy and the union hall setting.

event_blocks.summary: Harry Cripps, President of UAW Local 668, wearing his embroidered navy blue jacket, expressed concern in an empty union hall about the irreversible impact of automation and its future implications, as a sustained, low synth tone began. The scene shifted to a black screen where floating mathematical equations formed the title 'FOUR THE SURVEILLANCE CAPITALISTS'. Aerial shots then revealed Apple’s futuristic circular headquarters, complete with solar panels and greenery, and Google’s campus, characterized by modern glass buildings, colorful outdoor seating, and a vibrant multi-colored complex near a highway, zooming into a rooftop patio with blue and yellow umbrellas. A male narrator elaborated that a few highly successful tech companies are shaping the future by acquiring smaller firms and recruiting top AI talent, employing a significant portion of leading AI researchers and scientists in the US and China, and have become some of the world's wealthiest corporations. The segment concluded with Kai-Fu Lee, dressed in a dark blue suit and patterned tie, speaking thoughtfully in a softly lit room with a blurred bookshelf background and the FRONTLINE logo, asserting that 'AI really is the ultimate tool of wealth creation.'

Apple Building.description: Aerial view of Apple’s circular headquarters, a futuristic ring-shaped structure with solar panels and a central green space featuring trees, pathways, and a pond.

Harry Cripps.description: Man with a beard and glasses, wearing a navy blue zip-up jacket with 'PRESIDENT' and 'UAW LOCAL 668' embroidered on it, speaking in an empty union hall with stacked chairs and American flags in the background, expressing concern about automation's future impact.

Google Building.description: Aerial view of Google’s campus, featuring modern glass buildings with solar panels, green rooftops, and outdoor areas with colorful umbrellas and landscaping.

Kai-Fu Lee.description: Man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark blue suit, light blue shirt, and a patterned tie, speaking expressively in a softly lit room with a blurred bookshelf background; the FRONTLINE logo appears in the lower-left corner, explaining that AI is the "ultimate tool of wealth creation."

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of Harry Cripps, President of UAW Local 668, wearing his embroidered navy blue jacket, speaking with concern in an empty union hall about automation's irreversible impact and where it "leads us in the future." As a sustained, low synth tone begins, the scene transitions to a black screen with floating mathematical equations that coalesce into the title 'FOUR THE SURVEILLANCE CAPITALISTS'. Sweeping aerial shots then showcase Apple’s futuristic circular headquarters with solar panels and lush greenery, followed by Google’s campus featuring modern glass buildings, colorful outdoor seating, and a vibrant multi-colored complex near a highway, zooming to a rooftop patio with blue and yellow umbrellas. A male narrator explains that "the future is largely being shaped by a few hugely successful tech companies" that are "constantly buying up successful smaller companies and recruiting talent," employing "a great majority of the leading AI researchers and scientists" between the US and China, and have become "among the richest companies in the world." The segment concludes with a close-up of Kai-Fu Lee, in a dark blue suit and patterned tie, speaking thoughtfully in a softly lit room with a blurred bookshelf background and the FRONTLINE logo, stating that "AI really is the ultimate tool of wealth creation."

event_blocks.summary: In a softly lit room, Kai-Fu Lee, dressed in a dark blue suit and patterned tie, explained how companies like Facebook, Google, and Amazon utilize vast user data and AI to optimize advertising and revenue. The scene shifted to a dimly lit lecture hall where Pedro Domingos, author of 'The Master Algorithm,' wrote complex mathematical equations on a whiteboard, while a narrator noted growing public unease about how these algorithms infiltrate daily life and democracy. Domingos, identified with the University of Washington, then directly addressed the camera, gesturing animatedly as he described how personal data trails are constantly generated and analyzed by computers, warning that while algorithms aim to personalize our world, those controlling them might not share our objectives, emphasizing the need for awareness and control. Later, Shoshana Zuboff, a Harvard Business School Professor Emerita and author, seated in a warmly lit, book-filled study, reflected on the shift from being users of technology to being 'used' and 'searched' by it. Surrounded by books, papers, and lamps in a room with red walls and wooden bookshelves, she elaborated on 'surveillance capitalism,' drawing parallels to industrial capitalism's appropriation of nature and labor, but with a 'dark and startling twist' of claiming 'private human experience' as a free raw material. She spoke with conviction, wearing glasses, gold hoop earrings, and a heart-shaped pendant, her curly dark hair framing her face as she gestured expressively.

Pedro Domingos.description: Man with gray hair, wearing a dark suit and white shirt, speaking in an interview setting with the FRONTLINE logo and his affiliation (Univ. of Washington) displayed on screen; he is shown writing equations on a whiteboard and then speaking directly to the camera with animated gestures, explaining how computers personalize and adapt the world to us, but warning of the danger that companies controlling algorithms may not share our goals, urging awareness for control.

Kai-Fu Lee.description: Man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark blue suit, light blue shirt, and a patterned tie, speaking expressively in a softly lit room with a blurred bookshelf background. He articulates in a clear voice with a slight accent how companies like Facebook, Google, and Amazon use AI and massive user data to maximize profit.

Woman in Blue Sweater.description: Woman with curly dark hair, wearing glasses with thin frames, large gold hoop earrings, and a navy blue ribbed sweater with a gold heart-shaped pendant, seated in a warmly lit, book-filled study, speaking directly to the camera with a clear, articulate, thoughtful, and academic tone, discussing economic concepts like industrial and surveillance capitalism, and how they claim nature, work, and private human experience, while gesturing expressively with her hands.

raw_segments: The video opens with Kai-Fu Lee, a man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark blue suit, light blue shirt, and a patterned tie, speaking expressively in a softly lit room with a blurred bookshelf background. He articulates how companies like Facebook, Google, and Amazon leverage massive user data and AI to smartly target ads, maximize objective functions, and ultimately 'make more money.' A FRONTLINE logo is visible in the lower-left corner. The scene then transitions to a different setting, showing a man with gray hair, wearing a dark suit and white shirt, writing complex mathematical equations on a whiteboard in what appears to be a dim lecture hall or classroom. A narrator's voice explains that how these companies make money and how their algorithms reach deeper into our work, daily lives, and democracy makes many people increasingly uncomfortable. The narrator then identifies the man as Pedro Domingos, author of 'The Master Algorithm.' The video cuts to a close-up of Pedro Domingos, who is now speaking directly to the camera with animated hand gestures, his expression serious and focused, explaining that 'everywhere you go, you generate a cloud of data, you're trailing data, everything that you do is producing data. And then there are computers looking at that data.' His affiliation, 'Univ. of Washington,' is displayed on screen along with the FRONTLINE logo.

The segment opens with Pedro Domingos, a man with gray hair in a dark suit and white shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting, with the FRONTLINE logo visible. He gestures expressively as he explains how computers try to serve us better by personalizing and adapting the world to us, but warns of the danger that entities controlling these algorithms may not share our goals, urging awareness for control. He is also seen writing the mathematical equation 'p(x) = 1/2 e^(-x^2)' on a whiteboard. The scene then cuts to Shoshana Zuboff, a woman with curly dark hair, glasses, and a navy blue sweater, seated in a warmly lit, book-filled study. She speaks animatedly, gesturing with her hands, reflecting on how we entered this new world thinking we were users of social media and Google, only to realize they were actually 'using us' and 'searching us.' The camera pulls back to reveal her working at a large wooden desk with a computer, surrounded by stacks of papers, books, and lamps, in a room with red walls, extensive wooden bookshelves, and large windows. A narrator identifies her as a Harvard Business School Professor Emerita and author of the 1988 book 'In the Age of the Smart Machine,' noting her work over the last seven years.

The video opens with a medium shot of a woman in a navy blue ribbed sweater, seated at a large wooden desk in a warmly lit, book-filled study, actively typing on a keyboard. The camera then pans left to reveal a tall stack of books, including 'The Age of Surveillance Capitalism,' 'My Life and Work,' and 'The Visible Hand,' setting a scholarly tone. A male narrator's voice introduces a new book making the case for a "new phase of the economy" called "surveillance capitalism." The scene transitions to a close-up of the woman, who now speaks directly to the camera with a clear, articulate, thoughtful, and academic tone. Wearing glasses with thin frames, large gold hoop earrings, and a delicate gold heart-shaped pendant, her curly dark hair frames her face as she gestures expressively. She explains how "famously, industrial capitalism claimed nature" and "claimed work" for the market dynamic, to be "reborn as real estate" or "labor." She then introduces "surveillance capitalism" as following this pattern "but with a dark and startling twist," claiming "private human experience" as a "free source of raw material." The consistent background of wooden bookshelves, a lamp, and a fireplace, along with the soft, warm lighting, reinforces the intimate and academic ambiance as she speaks with conviction and engagement.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a Woman in Blue Sweater in a book-filled study, explaining how data is "fabricated into predictions of human behavior" for businesses seeking insight into future actions. This is followed by a nighttime exterior shot of a house, where Alistair McTaggart is seen looking at an object, illuminated by a warm glow, as a male narrator notes his unawareness of a "new surveillance business" until 2015. McTaggart, in a dark vest over a light blue shirt, is then shown in a close-up interview, recalling a conversation with an engineer about privacy concerns and the engineer's chilling remark, "you'd be horrified if you knew how much we knew about you." The scene shifts to McTaggart in a dimly lit room, watching a television, explaining that companies' "entire business is learning as much about you as they can," mining "everything about your thoughts and your desires and your dreams." Identified as a real estate developer, McTaggart's research into this business model led him to realize "their entire business is basically mining the data of your life." The setting then changes to a room filled with archival boxes labeled 'Internet of Things,' 'IoT Biz,' 'Big Data,' and 'Inevitable IoT,' where Shoshana Zuboff, wearing a dark blue long-sleeve top and gold pendant, sorts through boxes, detailing her years of research into patents, earnings calls, and reports, including the early days of Google starting in 1998. A melancholic string melody plays as the scene shows a colorful Google logo, followed by the PBS and FRONTLINE logos. The video then details Google's founding in 1998 by Sergey Brin and Larry Page, who initially lacked a clear business model and had an unofficial motto of 'don't be evil,' shown in photos and a server room. Despite public opposition to advertising from the founders, who believed it would 'distort the internet,' the dot-com crash of the early 2000s pressured companies to monetize. A Google logo with a doodle is displayed, alongside a classroom scene, as Google rapidly became the default search engine. In a modern office, men discuss the pressure for companies to make more money without a new business model. Shoshana Zuboff, identified as 'Author, The Age of Surveillance Capitalism,' explains that founders, in an emergency, discovered the value of "digital traces" left by users, visually represented by hands typing and a Google search interface. A male narrator explains how machine learning algorithms were applied to predict user interests, and Zuboff describes how these data logs were systematically utilized.

Woman in Blue Sweater.description: Woman with curly dark hair, wearing glasses with thin frames, large gold hoop earrings, a navy blue ribbed sweater, and a gold heart-shaped pendant, seated in a warmly lit study with bookshelves in the background, speaking expressively and articulately about digital traces and data logs, identified as Shoshana Zuboff, Author, The Age of Surveillance Capitalism.

Man in Dark Vest.description: McTaggart, a successful California real estate developer, with dark, neatly combed hair, wearing a dark blue quilted vest with a brown collar over a light blue button-down shirt, speaking with a serious and contemplative expression in a softly lit indoor setting with a blurred wooden background, explaining that companies' "entire business is learning as much about you as they can," and that "their entire business is basically mining the data of your life."

Archival Boxes.description: Stacks of gray archival boxes labeled with topics such as 'Internet of Things,' 'IoT Biz,' 'Big Data,' and 'Inevitable IoT,' taped with brown paper, arranged on a wooden floor in a room with wooden paneling and a leather armchair, suggesting a research or documentary setting.

Woman in Dark Blue Top.description: Shoshana Zuboff, a woman with dark, curly hair, wearing a dark blue long-sleeve top with shoulder cutouts and a gold pendant necklace, speaking about her extensive research, having been "reading and reading and reading from patents to transcripts of earnings calls, research reports," and "literally everything for years and years and years."

PBS.description: White circular logo with 'PBS' text, appearing briefly in the lower-left corner during the outdoor building shot, indicating the broadcasting network.

FRONTLINE Logo.description: Bold white 3D text on a red background, appearing in the lower-left corner during the interview scene, indicating the documentary source.

Google Building.description: Aerial view of Google’s campus, featuring modern glass buildings with solar panels, green rooftops, and outdoor areas with colorful umbrellas and landscaping.

Man in Maroon Shirt.description: Man with dark hair, wearing a maroon button-down shirt and dark pants, standing beside the man in the gray t-shirt, later seen with arms crossed in a server room; inferred to be one of the founders, Sergey Brin or Larry Page, as the narrator discusses Google's origins.

Classroom Scene.description: Room with students seated at wooden desks, one in a white shirt, another in a black shirt, with a whiteboard and colorful balloons in the background, suggesting a learning environment.

Man in Striped Shirt (Speaking).description: Man with dark hair, wearing a maroon and white striped shirt, speaking at a clear podium with a 'current' backdrop, gesturing with his right hand.

Man in Gray T-Shirt.description: Man with dark hair, wearing a gray t-shirt and dark pants, standing beside another man in front of a colorful geometric wall, holding a small object; inferred to be one of the founders, Sergey Brin or Larry Page, as the narrator discusses Google's origins.

Server Room.description: Long corridor of black server racks with yellow front panels, labeled with 'Google' banners above, creating a high-tech ambiance.

Google Logo.description: Bold, multicolored letters spelling 'Google' on a white background, appearing in a close-up shot of a search interface.

Computer Monitor (Google Doodle).description: Old-style CRT monitor displaying the Google logo with a doodle of a sun and the equation 'E=mc²' written beside it.

Tech Trade Show.description: Large indoor exhibition hall with multiple booths, including 'GLOBIX', 'MICROCHIP', and 'Software', featuring red carpets, banners, and attendees walking around.

Google Search Interface.description: Computer screen displaying the Google search bar with autocomplete suggestions for queries such as 'recipes for gumbo' and 'what was carol brady's maiden name', with the Google logo at the top.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a close-up of the Woman in Blue Sweater, seated in a warmly lit, book-filled study, wearing a navy blue ribbed sweater, large gold hoop earrings, and a gold heart-shaped pendant. Her curly dark hair frames her face as she speaks expressively, gesturing subtly, explaining how data is "fabricated into predictions of human behavior," noting that "a lot of businesses that really want to know what we will do now, soon, and later." An eerie, melancholic string/synth sound begins as the scene cuts to a dark, nighttime exterior shot of a house. Through a window, a man is visible inside, looking down at a small object, illuminated by a warm glow. A male narrator states, "Like most people, Alistair McTaggart had no idea about this new surveillance business until one evening in 2015." The camera zooms in on the window, showing the man, later identified as Alistair McTaggart, in a dark vest over a light blue shirt, his expression focused. The scene transitions to a close-up interview shot of Alistair McTaggart, now in a softly lit indoor setting with a blurred wooden background. He speaks with a serious, contemplative expression, his dark hair neatly combed, recalling a conversation with an engineer about privacy, asking "what's the big deal about all why why are people so worked up about it," and using an analogy about airline pilots before concluding with the engineer's chilling remark, "you'd be horrified if you knew how much we knew about you."

The scene opens in a dimly lit room with a low, sustained synth sound, where McTaggart, a man in a dark blue quilted vest over a light blue shirt, stands in profile, partially obscured by a wooden door frame, watching a television screen. Out-of-focus hanging glass pendant lights create a warm foreground as the camera slowly zooms in on his serious, contemplative face. He turns to speak, explaining that he learned companies' "entire business is learning as much about you as they can," mining "everything about your thoughts and your desires and your dreams." A narrator then identifies him as a successful California real estate developer, McTaggart, whose initial question led him to research this new business model. The shot cuts to a close-up of McTaggart's face as he continues, stating he didn't realize at the time that "their entire business is basically mining the data of your life." The setting shifts to a room filled with gray archival boxes labeled 'Internet of Things,' 'IoT Biz,' 'Big Data,' and 'Inevitable IoT.' A woman with dark, curly hair, identified by the narrator as Shoshana Zuboff, wearing a dark blue long-sleeve top with shoulder cutouts and a gold pendant necklace, is seen sorting through one of the boxes. She speaks about her extensive research, having been "reading and reading and reading from patents to transcripts of earnings calls, research reports, you know, just literally everything for years and years and years." The narrator adds that her studies included the early days of Google, which started in 1998. The synth sound fades, replaced by a melancholic string melody, as the final shot shows a colorful Google logo on a wall, with two smiling people partially visible, before the PBS and FRONTLINE logos appear.

The video opens with a close-up of the Google logo, as a male narrator introduces its founding in 1998 by two young Stanford grad students, Sergey Brin and Larry Page, who initially had no clear business model and an unofficial motto of 'don't be evil.' This narration accompanies a photo of two men, presumably Brin and Page, standing in front of a colorful, geometric wall, one holding a small object, followed by a scene of the same two men with arms crossed in a server room filled with yellow server racks and Google banners. The scene then shifts to a man speaking at a podium with a 'current' backdrop, but the audio features a woman's voice, later identified as the woman seen, discussing how the founders, Larry Page and Sergey Brin, were publicly against advertising, believing it would 'distort the internet' and 'disfigure the purity of any search engine.' This woman, with curly dark hair, glasses, and a navy blue ribbed sweater, is then shown speaking expressively in a warmly lit study with bookshelves. The audio transitions to a different male narrator describing the dot-com crash of the early 2000s, which left many internet companies struggling, as the visuals show a bustling tech trade show with booths for 'GLOBIX', 'MICROCHIP', and 'Software', and attendees on a red carpet. A close-up of a computer monitor displaying the Google logo with a doodle and 'E=mc²' is shown, followed by a classroom scene with students at desks and colorful balloons, while the narrator concludes that Google rapidly became the default search engine for millions of users. Throughout, a reflective background music track plays.

The video opens in a modern office where a group of men, including one thoughtful in a white shirt and another intent in a black shirt, are seated around a long conference table, engaged in a discussion. A male narrator's voice explains how companies were pressured to make more money and faced danger without a new business model. The scene then transitions to a warmly lit study where Shoshana Zuboff, identified by on-screen text as 'Author, The Age of Surveillance Capitalism' with the 'FRONTLINE' logo, speaks directly to the camera. With curly dark hair, glasses, large gold hoop earrings, a navy blue ribbed sweater, and a gold heart-shaped pendant, she articulately explains how founders, in an emergency, realized they needed to save their company, leading to discoveries about the "digital traces" left behind when people search or browse. This is visually reinforced by a close-up of hands typing on a keyboard, accompanied by typing sounds, followed by a Google search interface displaying queries like 'recipes for gumbo' and 'what was carol brady's maiden name.' The male narrator's voice then interjects, detailing how the value of this data was realized by applying machine learning algorithms to predict user interests. Shoshana Zuboff, maintaining eye contact and using hand gestures against a backdrop of bookshelves, continues her explanation, describing how they systematically turned to and began to use these data logs.

event_blocks.summary: A woman in a navy blue ribbed sweater, large gold hoop earrings, and glasses, seated in a warmly lit study with bookshelves, spoke expressively about using "plus data" to make "fine-grained predictions of what a user would click on, what kind of ad a user would click on," noting that "inside Google, they started seeing these revenues pile up at a startling rate," leading them to "keep it secret" because "they didn't want anyone to know how much money they were making or how they were making it," as "users had no idea that these extra behavioral data... was just out there and now it was being used to predict their future." The scene briefly cut to a glass-walled conference room where several men, including one in a maroon shirt with a lanyard and another in a dark jacket, listened intently to a presentation, before returning to the woman, who continued her speech with subtle hand movements. As dramatic, slightly suspenseful orchestral music began, the video shifted to a bustling Times Square, featuring yellow taxis and towering digital billboards, one prominently displaying 'WELCOME GOOGLE' and the Google logo. This transitioned to a historical moment at the NASDAQ, where a man in a suit rang the opening bell for Google’s IPO, surrounded by smiling executives, with a large screen showing the Google logo and stock data. A male narrator stated that "When Google's IPO took place just a few years later, the company had a market capitalization of around $23 billion," and "Google stock was now as valuable as General Motors." The woman's voice briefly returned, saying, "And it was only when Google..." before the segment concluded with an aerial view of a modern Google campus, showcasing glass buildings, a large white 'Google' sign, and an outdoor plaza with colorful umbrellas and greenery. The segment later featured the woman in the navy blue ribbed sweater speaking about Google's financial growth, stating that after going public in 2004, its revenue increased by 3,590% between 2000 and 2004. The scene transitioned to a formal panel discussion at the Newseum, where a man in a dark suit, light blue tie, and glasses gestured animatedly while speaking, asking, "let's talk a little bit about information and search and how people consume it," and explaining Google's predictive capabilities: "we don't need you to type at all. Because we know where you are... We know where you've been... We can more or less guess what you're thinking about," before questioning, "Now, is that over the line?" The man next to him, in a dark tie, listened attentively. A male narrator interjected, mentioning that by 2010, Google CEO Eric Schmidt would discuss these topics with The Atlantic, and that Schmidt and Google declined to be interviewed for the program, adding that Google's new business model for predicting user profiles migrated to other companies like Facebook, and that Roger McNamee was an early investor. Visually, an aerial view of a modern Google campus with glass buildings and solar panels was shown, followed by a bustling indoor professional networking event where attendees mingled, and one man in a dark suit held a drink while conversing, all accompanied by understated, slightly dramatic background music.

Man in Maroon Shirt.description: Man with dark hair, wearing a maroon button-down shirt and dark pants, seated in a conference room, listening intently with a lanyard around his neck.

Woman in Blue Sweater.description: Woman with curly dark hair, wearing glasses with thin frames, large gold hoop earrings, a navy blue ribbed sweater, and a gold heart-shaped pendant, seated in a warmly lit study with bookshelves in the background, speaking expressively about Google's revenue increasing by 3,590% between 2000 and 2004.

Google IPO Ceremony.description: Formal event at NASDAQ with a man in a suit ringing the opening bell, surrounded by executives, with a large screen displaying the Google logo and stock performance data, described by a male narrator as having occurred 'just a few years later' with a market capitalization of 'around $23 billion,' making Google stock 'as valuable as General Motors.'

Google Building.description: Aerial view of Google’s campus, featuring modern glass buildings with solar panels, green rooftops, and the 'Google' logo prominently displayed on a dark glass facade.

Man in Dark Jacket.description: Man with dark, wavy hair, wearing a dark jacket over a green shirt, seated in a conference room, resting his chin on his hand with a thoughtful expression.

Times Square.description: Bustling urban intersection with yellow taxis, towering digital billboards, and crowds, including a prominent 'WELCOME GOOGLE' sign and a large Google logo display.

Google Logo.description: Bold, multicolored letters spelling 'Google' on a white background, appearing on a large screen during the IPO event and on the campus sign.

NASDAQ.description: Large electronic display showing 'Google +14.0%' and stock data, with the NASDAQ logo prominently visible, during the IPO ceremony, which a male narrator details as the event where Google achieved a market capitalization of around $23 billion, with its stock becoming as valuable as General Motors.

Man in Suit (Speaking).description: Man with light brown hair, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and light blue tie, seated on stage at the Newseum, speaking animatedly with hand gestures, glasses on, and a microphone clipped to his lapel, discussing information consumption, search, and Google's ability to predict user thoughts and location without typing.

Man in Suit (Serious Expression).description: Man with a bald head, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and dark tie, seated on stage at the Newseum, listening attentively with hands clasped, holding a notepad.

Man in Dark Suit (Networking).description: Man with gray hair, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and patterned tie, holding a drink and engaged in conversation with other attendees at a professional networking event.

raw_segments: The video opens with a woman in a navy blue ribbed sweater, large gold hoop earrings, and glasses, seated in a warmly lit study with bookshelves, speaking expressively about using "plus data" to make "fine-grained predictions of what a user would click on, what kind of ad a user would click on." She explains that "inside Google, they started seeing these revenues pile up at a startling rate," leading them to "keep it secret" because "they didn't want anyone to know how much money they were making or how they were making it," as "users had no idea that these extra behavioral data... was just out there and now it was being used to predict their future." The scene briefly cuts to a glass-walled conference room where several men, including one in a maroon shirt with a lanyard and another in a dark jacket, listen intently to a presentation, before returning to the woman, who continues her speech with subtle hand movements. As dramatic, slightly suspenseful orchestral music begins, the video shifts to a bustling Times Square, featuring yellow taxis and towering digital billboards, one prominently displaying 'WELCOME GOOGLE' and the Google logo. This transitions to a historical moment at the NASDAQ, where a man in a suit rings the opening bell for Google’s IPO, surrounded by smiling executives, with a large screen showing the Google logo and stock data. A male narrator states that "When Google's IPO took place just a few years later, the company had a market capitalization of around $23 billion," and "Google stock was now as valuable as General Motors." The woman's voice briefly returns, saying, "And it was only when Google..." before the segment concludes with an aerial view of a modern Google campus, showcasing glass buildings, a large white 'Google' sign, and an outdoor plaza with colorful umbrellas and greenery.

The segment opens with a woman in a navy blue ribbed sweater, large gold hoop earrings, and glasses, seated in a warmly lit study with bookshelves, speaking expressively about Google's financial growth, stating that after going public in 2004, its revenue increased by 3,590% between 2000 and 2004. The scene transitions to a formal panel discussion at the Newseum, where a man in a dark suit, light blue tie, and glasses gestures animatedly while speaking, asking, "let's talk a little bit about information and search and how people consume it." He continues, explaining Google's predictive capabilities: "we don't need you to type at all. Because we know where you are... We know where you've been... We can more or less guess what you're thinking about," before questioning, "Now, is that over the line?" The man next to him, in a dark tie, listens attentively. A male narrator interjects, mentioning that by 2010, Google CEO Eric Schmidt would discuss these topics with The Atlantic, and that Schmidt and Google declined to be interviewed for the program. The narrator adds that Google's new business model for predicting user profiles migrated to other companies like Facebook, and that Roger McNamee was an early investor. Visually, an aerial view of a modern Google campus with glass buildings and solar panels is shown, followed by a bustling indoor professional networking event where attendees mingle, and one man in a dark suit holds a drink while conversing. Throughout, an understated, slightly dramatic background music plays.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a display of Roger McNamee's book 'Zucked,' setting a critical tone for a presentation by the author, a former Facebook advisor. Addressing a large audience at round tables, he stands at a podium, explaining how companies like Facebook and Google collect vast amounts of personal data from credit card processors, credit rating services, and health apps, even sensitive details like menstrual cycles, to enable "behavioral prediction" for advertising. He emphasizes that "we gave technology a place in our lives that it had not earned." Later, in an interview setting, he reflects on how these tech giants have "cashed in" on societal trust. The scene shifts to visuals of a modern campus, an open-plan office with a 'HACK' sign, and a Samsung smartphone displaying a Facebook news feed. A voice explains the model: "provide a free service like Facebook, and in exchange, you collect the data of the millions who use it," noting "every sliver of information is valuable." The interface shows 'Like' and emoji reactions, and a woman in a blue sweater explains that the specificity of communication, not just the act of posting, yields valuable data. The narrative then details Facebook's 2010 "social contagion experiment" using AI to influence real-world behavior, aiming to increase voter turnout in the mid-term elections. This is illustrated with scenes of a political rally and a polling station, with Facebook offering a 'VOTE' button and an 'I Voted' feature to 61 million users, claiming to have "nudged 340,000 people to vote."

Book 'Zucked' by Roger McNamee.description: White book cover with bold black text 'Zucked' and a large thumbs-down icon, stacked on a table at the event, indicating a critical theme related to Facebook or tech ethics.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a table displaying multiple copies of 'Zucked' by Roger McNamee, a book featuring a prominent thumbs-down icon, visually reinforcing the critical perspective of the speaker. The scene then transitions to a formal conference or luncheon where a large audience, dressed in business attire, is seated at round tables, attentively listening to a man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and patterned tie. This man, identified through the audio as a former advisor to Facebook who is now a critic and author of the book 'Zucked', stands at a clear acrylic podium, gesturing expressively as he delivers his speech. He voices his concern about how widely companies like Facebook and Google cast their net for data, explaining how they acquire information from credit card processors, credit rating services, and even health and wellness apps, including sensitive details like women's menstrual cycles. The audience, including a woman in a bright pink blazer and another in a black blazer with gold hoop earrings, listens intently to his detailed explanation of "behavioral prediction" and its aim to remove uncertainty from advertising and marketing. The scene shifts to a close-up of the speaker, now seated, continuing his address with a serious and thoughtful demeanor, emphasizing that "we gave technology a place in our lives that it had not earned." The softly blurred background maintains focus on his intellectual discourse on technology, ethics, and corporate accountability.

A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit, light purple shirt, and patterned tie, speaks thoughtfully in an interview setting, reflecting on how society developed a sense of inevitability that things would always get better, and how industries like Facebook and Google have "cashed in" on the trust and goodwill earned in previous decades. A low, sustained synth-like background music begins as the scene transitions to an aerial view of a modern campus with green rooftops and a highway, then inside a spacious office with a large 'HACK' sign and a person seated at a desk. A computer screen displays a Facebook page with a political figure, followed by a close-up of glasses reflecting a digital interface, and a hand holding a Samsung smartphone showing a Facebook news feed with a Spotify playlist and a video of a man with a cat. A distinct male voice explains that the model is to "provide a free service like Facebook, and in exchange, you collect the data of the millions who use it," emphasizing that "every sliver of information is valuable." The hand taps the 'Share' button on the phone, showing Facebook's 'Like' and emoji reactions. Finally, a woman with curly dark hair, glasses, a navy blue ribbed sweater, and gold hoop earrings speaks expressively in a warmly lit study, gesturing as she highlights that it's not just *what* you post, but *that* you post, and the specificity of your communication, like saying "I'll see you later" versus "I'll see you at 6:45," that reveals valuable data.

The minute opens with a calm, authoritative female voice discussing 'tiny signals' and 'behavioral surplus' having 'immense predictive value,' accompanied by subtle, mysterious electronic music. Visually, a Facebook interface highlights a 'Like' button and 'Write about yourself' prompt, then a 'Join Group' button is clicked. A male narrator explains that in 2010, Facebook experimented with AI's predictive powers in a 'social contagion experiment' to influence real-world behavior through online messaging. The scene shifts to an aerial view of a modern campus and a bustling open-plan office with employees and a large globe, followed by a 'News Feed TEAM' sign. The narrator reveals the experiment's aim was 'to get more people to the polls in the 2010 mid-term elections.' This is visually represented by a political rally where a man in a suit walks onto a stage to a cheering crowd, with 'MOVING AMERICA FORWARD' signs, and is heard passionately urging, 'I need you to keep on fighting! I need you to keep on believing!' The video returns to a Facebook interface showing a 'VOTE' button and 'I Voted' feature, which the narrator states was offered to 61 million users. A polling station with voters at booths and a poll worker then appears, illustrating the civic participation Facebook claimed to have 'nudged 340,000 people to vote,' before the segment concludes back in the tech office with focused employees.

event_blocks.summary: A Facebook sponsored ad for Wayfair, showcasing a black leather sofa with 'Free Shipping on Everything' under the 'MYWAY' logo, is displayed on a computer screen, with a cursor clicking the 'Like' button, while a calm, authoritative male voice discusses "massive contagion experiments" designed to manipulate user emotions by altering their feeds. The scene briefly shifts to a candy display featuring a 'BOGO 50% off' promotion with brands like Starburst, Snickers, and M&M's. Subsequently, a woman with curly dark hair, wearing glasses, a navy blue ribbed sweater, gold hoop earrings, and a heart-shaped pendant, speaks expressively in a warmly lit study with bookshelves, gesturing with hands adorned with rings, including a prominent green stone ring. Her clear voice explains how "they" boasted about leveraging online cues to alter real-world behavior covertly, deeming it "big news." The video then transitions to a man with gray hair and glasses, dressed in a dark suit, light purple shirt, and patterned tie, speaking in a softly blurred interview setting. His serious, authoritative voice asserts that "private corporations have built a corporate surveillance state" without public awareness or consent, noting the increasing sophistication of systems like the "Internet of Things," as the camera zooms in on his focused expression.

The camera zooms in on the speaker, which glows red as a male voice discusses "appliances powered by the Alexa voice recognition system or the Google Home system." On-screen text 'Promotional video' appears in the top left corner. Another male voice then commands, "Okay Google, play the morning playlist," to which a female AI voice responds, "Okay, playing morning playlist," as gentle acoustic music begins. The scene transitions to a dimly lit bedroom where a young girl sleeps, her bedside table holding a small smart speaker, with the 'Promotional video' text still visible, while the male voice continues, "Okay Google, play music in all rooms." A different male narrator then discusses how these devices "put the surveillance in place as we've never had it before" in "living rooms, kitchens, bedrooms," finding it "terrifying." A female AI voice interjects, "Okay Google, I'm listening." The narrator continues, explaining that companies are not using data to target ads but to help AI "improve the user experience." The visuals shift to a woman in a dark blazer adjusting her jacket in a sunlit room, then a man in a gray shirt adjusting his tie, and a woman in a gray cardigan reading in a cozy living room. A close-up reveals a white smart speaker on a wooden cabinet beside a green fan, as a female voice commands, "Alexa, turn on the fan," and the AI responds, "Okay," followed by the distinct sound of a fan starting. The narrator returns, discussing research and patent applications to expand reach into homes. The video then shows a white smart doorbell with a blue ring light, and a hand holding a smartphone displaying a live video feed from the doorbell, while the female voice commands, "Alexa, take a video." Finally, a woman with curly hair and glasses speaks directly to the camera, explaining that "the more and more that you use spoken interfaces, so smart speakers, they're being trained not just to recognize..." The lighting throughout transitions from bright daylight to warm indoor tones, emphasizing a domestic, tech-integrated lifestyle.

event_blocks.summary: Amy Webb, Founder of the Future Today Institute, wearing black-rimmed glasses and a black leather jacket over a patterned blouse, explained that systems establish baselines and track changes over time, such as variations in cadence, sneezing, or voice wobbles, to infer real-time information about individuals, potentially identifying conditions like a cold, manic phase, or depression. The FRONTLINE logo appeared as she elaborated on the vast data obtainable from simple interactions, like asking an Amazon Echo smart speaker about the weather. Subsequently, a woman in a dark sleeveless top walked past a white Amazon Echo on a brick wall, near a floral arrangement. A voice asked, "Alexa, what's the weather for tonight?" to which the speaker responded, "Currently in Pasadena, it's 58 degrees with cloudy skies." The woman replied, "Inside it is then," followed by a child's shout of "Dinner!" The scene shifted to another interview where a woman in a navy blue ribbed sweater, gold hoop earrings, and a heart-shaped pendant, gesturing with rings including one with a green stone, spoke in a warmly lit study, stating, "The point is that this is the same micro behavioral targeting that..."

She has a clear, articulate voice, explaining how systems analyze personal data from speech patterns to infer health or emotional states, and the implications of smart speakers collecting such information.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of Amy Webb, Founder of the Future Today Institute, speaking in an interview setting. Wearing black-rimmed glasses, a black leather jacket over a patterned blouse, and silver hoop earrings, she gestures expressively with her hands as she articulately explains how systems are starting to take baselines and compare changes over time—such as whether your cadence increases or decreases, if you're sneezing while talking, or if your voice is a little wobbly—to understand more about you in real-time and make inferences, perhaps like if you have a cold, are in a manic phase, or feeling depressed. The FRONTLINE logo appears in the lower-left corner. Amy continues, stating that this is an extraordinary amount of information that can be gleaned by you simply waking up and asking your smart speaker "what's the weather today?" The scene then cuts to a woman in a dark sleeveless top walking past a white Amazon Echo smart speaker on a brick wall, with a white flower arrangement nearby. A woman's voice asks, "Alexa, what's the weather for tonight?" as the camera focuses on the speaker, which has a glowing blue ring at the top. A synthesized female voice responds, "Currently in Pasadena, it's 58 degrees with cloudy skies." The woman's voice then replies, "Inside it is then." A child's voice shouts "Dinner!" before the scene transitions to a different interview. Here, a woman in a navy blue ribbed sweater, gold hoop earrings, and a heart-shaped pendant speaks in a warmly lit study with bookshelves filled with books in the background. She gestures with her hands, adorned with rings including a green stone ring, as she speaks with a thoughtful, serious expression, stating, "The point is that this is the same micro behavioral targeting that..."

event_blocks.summary: In a warmly lit study, a Woman in Dark Blue Top, adorned with glasses, gold hoop earrings, and a heart pendant, explained how data was directed toward individuals based on intimate personality understanding, a tactic employed by Cambridge Analytica by pivoting from advertisers to political outcomes. The scene shifted to a formal hearing room, filled with the sounds of camera clicks and murmuring, where Mark Zuckerberg, in a dark suit and blue tie, navigated a dense crowd of photographers and journalists before being seated. A narrator detailed how the data of up to 87 million Facebook users was harvested by Cambridge Analytica, a UK-based political consulting firm funded by Robert Mercer, to target and manipulate voters in the 2016 US presidential campaign and the Brexit referendum. Kate Crawford, Co-founder of the AI Now Institute, wearing a black blazer, stated that despite Cambridge Analytica, the opposite of learning a lesson is true, warning that current tools and machine learning present a much bigger and more serious problem for manipulation of elections, opinions, and information travel than Cambridge Analytica's "old-school first-wave data science." The narrative then introduced AI pioneer Yoshua Bengio, a man with gray hair and a beard in a red shirt, who wrote complex equations on a transparent whiteboard and explained that AIs are tools that will serve those who control them, implying a potential conflict of interest if those interests are against the public's.

Woman in Dark Blue Top.description: Woman with curly dark hair, wearing black-rimmed glasses, a navy blue ribbed sweater, large gold hoop earrings, and a heart-shaped pendant, speaking expressively in a warmly lit study with bookshelves in the background, gesturing with hands adorned with rings including a green stone ring. She speaks with a clear, articulate voice, warning about billionaires using data and surveillance capitalism to upend democracy.

He then speaks, apologizing for not taking a broad enough view of responsibility and admitting it was his mistake. A narrator further details his apologies for privacy violations, his company's $5 billion fine, and Facebook's commitment to data protection and suspension of third-party apps.

FRONTLINE Logo.description: Bold white 3D text on a red background, appearing in the lower-left corner during the interview scene, indicating the documentary source.

Kate Crawford.description: Kate Crawford, Co-founder of the AI Now Institute at NYU, a woman with red hair, wearing a black blazer over a black top, speaks expressively with hand gestures in a softly lit indoor interview setting, discussing the lessons not learned from Cambridge Analytica and the more serious manipulation capabilities of current machine learning tools.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a woman in a dark blue ribbed sweater, wearing black-rimmed glasses, large gold hoop earrings, and a heart-shaped pendant necklace, speaking expressively in a warmly lit study with bookshelves in the background. Her clear voice explains how data was "directed toward individuals based on intimate, detailed understanding of personalities," stating, "this is precisely what Cambridge Analytica did," by "simply pivoting from the advertisers to the political outcomes." The scene then cuts to a wide shot of a formal hearing room, where the sounds of camera clicks and murmuring fill the air as a male narrator's voice begins, detailing how "The Cambridge Analytica scandal of 2018 engulfed Facebook, forcing Mark Zuckerberg to appear before Congress." Mark Zuckerberg, in a dark suit and blue tie, walks through the dense crowd of photographers and journalists, surrounded by security and media, before being seated at the central table, facing the press amidst intense camera flashes. The narrator continues, explaining how "the data of up to 87 million Facebook users had been harvested by a political consulting company based in the UK," with the "purpose... to target and manipulate voters in the 2016 presidential campaign, as well as the Brexit referendum," and that "Cambridge Analytica had been largely funded by conservative hedge fund billionaire Robert Mercer." Amidst this public scrutiny, the scene shifts to a man with white hair, wearing a dark suit, striped shirt, and patterned tie, holding a phone to his ear in front of a blurred abstract painting, suggesting a moment of private communication. The woman's voice then returns, stating, "And now we know that..."

The video opens with a close-up of a Woman in Dark Blue Top, wearing a navy blue ribbed sweater, black-rimmed glasses, large gold hoop earrings, and a heart-shaped pendant, her curly dark hair framing her face. In a warmly lit study with wooden bookshelves and a fireplace, she speaks expressively, gesturing with her ring-adorned hands, including a prominent green stone ring. Her clear, articulate voice warns that "any billionaire with enough money" can "buy the data, buy the machine intelligence capabilities, buy the skilled data scientists," and "they too can commandeer the public and infect and infiltrate and upend our democracy with the same methodologies that surveillance capitalism uses every single day." The scene then cuts to a formal hearing room where Mark Zuckerberg, in a dark suit and blue tie, sits at a wooden desk with a microphone, surrounded by an audience in business attire. With a serious expression, he listens, then speaks, stating, "We didn't take a broad enough view of our responsibility and that was a big mistake. And it was my mistake, and I'm sorry." A male narrator then details that Zuckerberg has apologized for numerous privacy violations, and his company was recently fined $5 billion by the Federal Trade Commission. He has said Facebook will now make data protection a priority and has suspended tens of thousands of third-party apps from its platform. The video concludes with an aerial view of a modern campus, featuring a large courtyard with 'HACK' spelled out in gray tiles, surrounded by buildings with solar panels and greenery, suggesting a tech company headquarters.

The segment opens with an aerial view of a modern tech campus, featuring a large courtyard with 'HACK' spelled out in gray tiles, surrounded by buildings with solar panels and greenery, establishing an innovative environment. The scene transitions to an indoor interview setting where Kate Crawford, Co-founder of the AI Now Institute at NYU, speaks expressively with hand gestures, wearing a black blazer over a black top, her red hair framing her face, against a softly lit background, with the FRONTLINE logo visible. She states, "I wish I could say that after Cambridge Analytica, we've learned our lesson... but I'm afraid the opposite is true." Crawford explains that Cambridge Analytica used "old-school first-wave data science," but warns that "what we're looking at now with current tools and machine learning is the ability for manipulation, both in terms of elections and opinions, but more broadly, just how information travels. That is a much bigger problem and certainly much more serious." A narrator then introduces "AI pioneer Yoshua Bengio," who also has concerns. The video cuts to Yoshua Bengio, a man with gray hair and a beard, wearing a red collared shirt, writing complex equations and terms like 'constant' and 'tangent' on a transparent whiteboard with a blue marker. He then turns to the camera, maintaining a serious demeanor, and explains, "So the AIs are tools, and they will serve the people who control those tools. If those people's interests go against the..." as his statement trails off.

event_blocks.summary: A man with gray hair and a beard, wearing a red shirt, discusses the responsibility of scientists to consider societal impact, comparing computer scientists working on AI to physicists warning about nuclear power during WWII. He writes equations on a transparent whiteboard as orchestral music begins, transitioning to a night view of the San Francisco skyline. A narrator introduces Alister MacTaggart, an ordinary citizen alarmed by corporate data collection, who launched a signature drive for a California ballot initiative to give consumers control over their digital data, spending $4 million of his own money. MacTaggart, seen on a stage with a cityscape backdrop, explains how companies identify individuals based on personal information and their 'frictionless way of communicating,' calling it 'power.' Screenshots of sensational political headlines from Facebook and news sites illustrate the context. The narrator details MacTaggart's effort to gather signatures, facing opposition from AT&T and Comcast, and notes that polls showed 80% approval for a privacy law, prompting politicians to pay attention. MacTaggart, later in a suit in front of the U.S. Capitol Building, negotiates with these companies, proposing to withdraw the initiative if a new law, AB375, is passed and signed by the governor by a specific deadline. The video shows an aerial view of a suburban cityscape at sunset, then focuses on a man in a dark blue vest expressing fear of facing companies with a combined market cap over six trillion dollars. The Legislative Assembly Chamber is shown with a digital display for AB375. The man in the vest narrates that a Republican vote was unexpected due to the bill including a private right of action for consumers. A vote tally shows AB 375 passing unanimously with 36 'yes' votes. The man expresses his astonishment and calls it an unforgettable day. A podium scene shows four individuals posing, and the man in the vest concludes that in January, Californians will have the 'right to know' what information companies have collected and the 'right to say no.'

Man with Gray Hair and Beard (Red Shirt).description: Man with gray hair and a beard, wearing a red collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, articulate voice about the responsibility of scientists and comparing computer scientists to physicists of the past; he gestures expressively while writing mathematical equations like 'constant' and 'tangent' on a transparent whiteboard with a blue marker.

Man in Checkered Shirt.description: Man with short dark hair, wearing a light blue checkered shirt and khaki pants, identified by a narrator as McTaggart, standing on a stage with a dark abstract cityscape backdrop, holding a remote and gesturing as he speaks about companies' intimate knowledge of individuals, access to personal information, and 'power.' He is later seen seated at a formal meeting table with microphones and standing confidently in front of the U.S. Capitol Building, as the narrator details his initiative to give consumers control of their digital data and his efforts to 'rein in the Goliaths of Silicon Valley.'

San Francisco Skyline.description: Nighttime view of the San Francisco skyline across the bay, featuring illuminated buildings including the Salesforce Tower, the Golden Gate Bridge, and Coit Tower on Telegraph Hill, with calm water reflecting the city lights.

Political Headline Screenshot.description: Series of web screenshots showing sensational political headlines from Facebook and news sites, including 'FBI Leak: Hillary Clinton Foundation Guilty Of Treason' and 'Pope Benedict XVI Forbids Catholics From Voting for Hillary!', with bold text and images of political figures, visually representing the kind of content or influence that McTaggart's initiative aims to address regarding digital data control.

U.S. Capitol Building.description: Nighttime view of the U.S. Capitol with illuminated windows and an American flag waving, symbolizing governmental authority.

Formal Meeting Scene.description: Man in Checkered Shirt seated at a long table with microphones, surrounded by other attendees, in a formal setting with wood paneling and documents, indicating a committee or hearing.

Facebook Interface.description: Computer screen displaying a sponsored ad for Wayfair with a black leather sofa and 'Free Shipping on Everything' under the 'MYWAY' logo, followed by a close-up of a 'Like' button being clicked.

Formal Hearing Room.description: Interior room with a large wooden table, individuals in business attire seated with laptops and documents, and a woman standing to address the group, where a female voice is heard instructing attendees to 'Please take your seats' and addressing a 'Mr. Secretary', suggesting a formal legislative or committee meeting.

Capitol Hallway.description: Long, arched hallway with cream-colored walls, decorative sconces, and a patterned carpet, featuring a green directional sign listing '1st FLOOR' and 'BASEMENT' amenities.

Capitol Building.description: Grand white neoclassical building with a central dome, surrounded by green trees and urban structures, featuring prominent columns and ornate architectural details, viewed from multiple aerial and close-up angles.

FRONTLINE Logo.description: Bold white 3D text on a red background, appearing in the lower-left corner during the aerial cityscape shot, indicating the documentary source.

Legislative Assembly Chamber.description: Grand hall with red carpeting, wooden desks, American and California flags, and a digital display showing 'Assembly 3rd Reading AB375 Chau et al. By Hertzberg' with a timer. Members are seated or moving, some using laptops, and a man in a dark suit walks across the chamber.

Man in Dark Vest.description: Man with dark, neatly combed hair, wearing a dark blue quilted vest with a brown collar over a light blue button-down shirt, speaking with a serious and contemplative expression in a softly lit indoor setting with a blurred wooden background. He narrates the legislative proceedings, describing the voting on AB 375, expressing personal feelings of being 'blown away' by its unanimous passage, and explaining the bill's implications for Californian residents' 'right to know' and 'right to say no' regarding their personal information, all in a calm, thoughtful voice.

Podium Scene.description: Four individuals—two men and two women—posing together in front of a backdrop featuring the California State Capitol and flags. The man in the center wears a dark suit with a red tie, the man to his left wears a light blue shirt and yellow tie, the woman on the far left wears a dark brown dress, and the man on the far right wears a light gray suit with a striped tie.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a man with gray hair and a beard, wearing a red collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room with a whiteboard behind him. He articulately discusses the responsibility of scientists, stating, "the values of democracy, then democracy is in danger." He continues, "So I believe that scientists who contribute to science when that science can or will have an impact on society, those scientists have a responsibility." He gestures expressively as he talks, comparing modern computer scientists working on machine learning and AI to physicists of the Second World War who warned governments about nuclear power, noting, "nuclear power can be dangerous and nuclear war can be really, really destructive." The camera cuts to a close-up of his hand writing mathematical equations, including 'constant' and 'tangent', on a transparent whiteboard with a blue marker, alternating between his face and the writing. As he concludes his thought, a calm, slightly dramatic orchestral background music begins. The scene then transitions to a wide, serene night shot of the San Francisco skyline, with the illuminated Salesforce Tower and the Golden Gate Bridge visible across the water. The camera pans slowly across the cityscape, capturing the glowing lights of buildings and the Coit Tower on Telegraph Hill, accompanied by the continuing music. A male narrator's voice overlays the scene, stating, "One person who wanted to do something about the dangers was not a computer scientist, but an ordinary citizen. Alister MacTaggart was alarmed." The scene shifts again to a man in a light blue checkered shirt standing on a stage with a dark, abstract cityscape backdrop, holding a remote and gesturing as he speaks. Identified as Alister MacTaggart, he states, "Voting is for me the most alarming one. If less than 100,000 votes separated..."

The video opens with the Man in Checkered Shirt, later identified by a narrator as McTaggart, standing on a stage with a dark, abstract cityscape backdrop, holding a remote and gesturing as he speaks. He articulates how companies intimately know individuals, identifying 'who's a racist, who's a misogynist, who's a homophobe, who's a conspiracy theorist,' and 'the lazy people and the gullible people,' emphasizing their 'access to the greatest trove of personal information' and 'frictionless way of communicating,' concluding, 'This is power.' The scene then transitions to a series of screenshots from Facebook and news websites, displaying sensational political headlines like 'FBI Leak: Hillary Clinton Foundation Guilty Of Treason' and 'Pope Benedict XVI Forbids Catholics From Voting for Hillary!', illustrating the type of content McTaggart is addressing.
